1: 'See — a king will reign in righteousness, and his rulers will govern with justice' — the messianic governance oracle: 'hineh lesedeq yimlokh-melek, behold a king will reign in righteousness.' The sedeq (righteousness/justice) as the defining attribute of the ideal king echoes Isaiah 11:4-5: 'with righteousness he will judge the needy... righteousness will be his belt.' The contrast with the current kings whose policy is oppression (Isaiah 30:12) and the Egypt alliance. 'And his rulers will govern with justice' (vesarim lemishpat yashoru, and princes for justice will govern) — the leadership team (sarim = princes/officials) also characterized by mishpat (justice). This pairs with Isaiah 28:6 (where YHWH gives 'a spirit of justice to those who sit in judgment'): the messianic era restores just governance at every level. Historically applied to Hezekiah's reform; eschatologically to the Davidic Messiah.
2: 'Each leader will be like a hiding place from the wind, a shelter from the storm — like streams of water in a dry land, like the shade of a great rock in a parched and weary ground' — the four shelter-images for ideal leadership. 'Vehayah ha'ish keseter ruach, and the man will be like a hiding place from the wind' (seter = a covered place, a hiding place, cf. Psalms 32:7: 'you are my hiding place'). 'And a shelter from the storm' (vesekher mizarem, a refuge from the rain/storm). 'Like streams of water in a dry place' (kefalge mayim betsayyon, like streams/channels of water in a dry land = the wadi-water in the Negev wilderness after rain). 'Like the shade of a great rock in a weary land' (ketsel sela kaved be'erets ayefah, like the shade of a heavy/great rock in a weary land) — four protective functions against four natural threats: wind, storm, thirst, scorching heat. The messianic leader as creation-shelter against creation's threats.
3: 'Then the eyes of those who see will no longer be closed, and the ears of those who hear will be attentive' — the reversal of the blindness-deafness of Isaiah 6:9-10 and Isaiah 29:9-12: 'velo tish'einah einei ro'im, and the eyes of those who see will not be dull/glazed.' Tish'einah (from sha'ah = to be smeared over, to be dull/glazed) = the physical-spiritual unseeing of those under the spirit of deep sleep (Isaiah 29:10). 'And the ears of those who hear will be attentive' (ve'ozne shome'im tiqshavna, and the ears of those who hear will be alert) — tiqshavna from qashav = to pay close attention, to be attentive (the same verb as Psalms 17:1: 'hear my righteous plea; listen/be attentive to my cry'). The complete reversal: those whose eyes saw nothing and ears heard nothing in the era of judgment now see and hear fully in the messianic era.
4: 'The heart of the impulsive will understand and know, and the tongue of those who stumbled over words will speak with clarity' — the cognitive-communicative restoration: 'velev nahum yavvein lada'at, and the heart of the rash/impulsive will understand to know.' The nahumm (rash/hasty, from nahm = to hasten recklessly) = those whose decision-making was impulsive and ill-considered (the opposite of the wise counsel of Isaiah 28:29). 'Will understand' (yavvein = the same root as beinah, understanding) 'to know' (lada'at) = the full cognitive restoration. 'And the tongue of those who stumbled over words will speak with clarity' (velashon ilgim temalel mahir, and the tongue of the stammerers will speak quickly/clearly). The ilgim (stammerers/those who speak poorly = the foreign-tongue invaders of Isaiah 28:11; also perhaps those unable to speak clearly in prophetic court) now speak mahir (quickly, fluently, clearly). The messianic era heals communication.
5: 'No longer will the fool be called noble, nor the miser praised as generous' — the reversal of false social labels: 'lo-yiqare od lenavel nadiv, no longer will the fool be called noble.' The naval (fool = the morally obtuse, the one who denies God's claim on his life, Psalms 14:1: 'the fool says in his heart there is no God'; Proverbs 17:7: 'lofty speech is not fitting for a fool'; 1 Samuel 25:25: Nabal/naval as a name-that-fits) called nadiv (noble = the generous, open-handed, spiritually exalted person). In the current age, the fool gets social credit for noble status. 'Nor will the miser be praised as generous' (velekilay lo ye'amer shoa, and the miser will not be said to be generous/bountiful) — shoa (bountiful/generous/noble = the prosperous person who gives freely) applied to the kilay (the knavish/fraudulent miser). The false labeling of the current social order corrected.
6: 'For the fool speaks foolishness, and his heart devises wickedness — practicing godlessness and speaking distortion about the LORD, leaving the hungry without food and cutting off drink from the thirsty' — the definition of the naval by his speech and deeds: 'ki naval nevulah yedabber velibbo ya'aseh-aven, for the fool speaks foolishness and his heart does/makes iniquity.' The naval's two domains: speech (nevulah = vileness/disgrace, from the same root as naval) and heart-deeds (aven = iniquity/trouble). 'Practicing godlessness' (la'asot chanef, to do profaneness/godlessness — chanef = the polluted/profane person who violates sacred boundaries). 'And speaking distortion about YHWH' (vedabber el-YHWH tatuf, speaking concerning YHWH distortion/error) = false theology that misrepresents YHWH's character. The social consequences: 'to leave the hungry without food' (lehariq nefesh ra'ev) and 'cut off drink from the thirsty' (umashqeh tsame yachser) — active deprivation of the vulnerable.
7: 'The miser's schemes are all evil; he devises wicked plans to ruin the poor with false words, even when the needy speak the truth' — the keli (miser/knave, the fraudulent dealer) analyzed: 'ukheli kelav re'im, and the tools/instruments of the knave are evil.' Keli (vessel/instrument, but also 'the knave himself') = the miser's whole apparatus of scheming. 'He devises wicked plans' (hu zimot yiyetsov leshachet anavim, he devises schemes to destroy the humble/poor) — the specific target: the anavim (the meek/poor, as in Isaiah 29:19). 'With false words' (bishfat-shaqer, with words of deception/falsehood). 'Even when the needy speak the truth' (ve'im-dabbur evyon mishpat, and when the poor man speaks justice/right). The miser's wickedness is in perverting justice even against those who speak the truth — the most blatant corruption of the gate-court (Isaiah 21:28-21's legal snares).
8: 'But the noble person plans noble things, and by noble deeds he stands firm' — the positive counter-definition of the nadiv (noble/generous): 'venediv nadivot ya'ats, and the noble person plans noble things.' The nadiv (the free, open-handed, generous spirit) makes nadivot (noble plans/generous plans). 'And on noble deeds he stands firm' (ve'hu al-nadivot yaqum, and he on noble deeds will stand firm). The standing-firm (yaqum = will rise/stand) of the noble: the nadiv's nobility is self-sustaining — the generous character that plans generously produces the stability that allows continued generosity. Contrast the schemes of the kali (Isaiah 32:7) that destroy — the nadiv's plans build. This is the social order of the messianic king's realm (Isaiah 32:1): justice produces the stable community where the noble can thrive and the poor are protected.
9: 'Rise up, you women who are at ease; hear my voice. Give attention to what I say, you complacent daughters' — the women's oracle paralleling the men's: 'nashim sha'ananot qumnah, women who are at ease, rise up; shime'u qoli, hear my voice.' The sha'ananot (women at ease/secure = complacent, comfortable) are addressed in prophetic counter-intuition: when society assumes safety, the prophet announces judgment. The parallel address: 'banot betachot, complacent daughters' (batachot = confident, secure, from batach = to trust, the misplaced trust that pervades Isaiah 28-31). This oracle echoes Amos 4:1-3 ('hear this word, you cows of Bashan on Mount Samaria — you women who oppress the poor and crush the needy and say to your husbands, Bring us some drinks!') where women are addressed as the barometers of societal complacency.
10: 'In little more than a year you complacent women will tremble, for the grape harvest will fail and the gathering will not come' — the harvest-failure oracle: 'yamim al-shanah tir'adna, days and a year you will tremble' (tir'adna = you (feminine plural) will be shuddering). 'Days and a year' = within a year and a few days (the indefinite 'soon but not immediately'). 'For the grape harvest will fail' (ki kalah batsiyr, for the vintage is finished/done = no vintage this year). 'And the gathering will not come' (ve'asaf lo yavo, and the fruit-gathering will not arrive). The grape harvest (batsir) was the great autumn celebration that marked the end of the agricultural year — its failure was an economic and social catastrophe. The complacent women who assumed continued prosperity will tremble when the harvest they counted on does not materialize.
11: 'Tremble, you women who are at ease; shudder, you who feel secure! Strip off your clothes, bare your body, and tie sackcloth around your waist' — the mourning ritual commands: 'rig'u sha'ananot, tremble/shudder, you at-ease ones' (rig'u from ragaz = to tremble/be disturbed). 'Vachargazu batahot, and shudder, you secure ones.' The commands intensify: from trembling to undressing for mourning rites. 'Strip off your clothes' (peshitah veoru, strip and make yourselves naked) = the stripping of mourning, the removal of status garments (cf. 2 Samuel 3:31: 'tear your clothes and put on sackcloth and walk in mourning before Abner'). 'Tie sackcloth around your waist' (vachagoru al-chalatsayim, and gird on the hips) — sackcloth (the rough goat-hair cloth of mourning, Genesis 37:34; 2 Samuel 3:31; Jonah 3:5-8) around the waist = the complete reversal from festival dress to mourning dress.
12: 'Beat your breasts in mourning for the pleasant fields and the fruitful vines' — the breast-beating mourning gesture: 'al-shadayim sof'dim, over the breasts beat in mourning' (sof'dim = beating the chest in grief, from safad = to wail/beat in mourning). The object of mourning: 'al-sadot chemad, over the pleasant/desirable fields' and 'al-gefen poriyah, over the fruitful vine.' The fields (sadot) and vines (gefen) are the two primary agricultural products of the land — their loss is the loss of the covenant blessing (Deuteronomy 8:8: 'a land with wheat and barley, vines and fig trees'). The women mourn over the agricultural landscape as if over the dead — because the failed harvest is as devastating as death for the covenant community's sustenance.
13: 'Thorns and briers will spring up on the soil of my people's land, on every house of celebration in the bustling city' — the covenant-curse vegetation: 'al-admat ammi, on the soil of my people' (admat ammi = the covenant land-of-my-people; adamah = the soil, the cultivated land given under covenant). 'Sha'amiyah kol-beit masos qiryah alitsah, thorns and briers will spring up — over every house of celebration, over the jubilant city.' The shamir vashayit (thorns and briers) = the judgment-vegetation of Isaiah 5:6; Isaiah 7:23-25; Isaiah 9:18; Isaiah 10:17; Isaiah 27:4; Isaiah 32:13 — the thorn-complex that signals the reversal of the cultivated covenant-land back to wilderness. The 'houses of celebration' (beit masos, houses of rejoicing = the places of the festival/banquet culture) abandoned, overgrown with thorns. The jubilant city silenced.
14: 'For the palace will be abandoned, the busy city deserted; the citadel and the watchtower will serve as lairs for wild donkeys and grazing ground for flocks — forever' — the complete depopulation: 'ki-armun nuttash, for the palace will be abandoned' (nuttash = forsaken/abandoned, the technical term for covenant-abandonment). 'Qiryah hamonah uzuvah, the crowded/bustling city forsaken.' The two markers of civilization (palace = royal governance; crowded city = commercial social life) both abandoned. 'The citadel and the watchtower will become caves/lairs forever' (ofel vavachan hayah mearot, the hill-citadel and watchtower will be for dens) — 'for wild donkeys' (ad-olam, to the age) = the permanent de-civilization of the site. 'Grazing ground for flocks' (mire'eh pere-chamor, grazing for the wild donkey) — the domestic city becomes wild pasture. The same imagery as Isaiah 13:20-22 (Babylon) and Isaiah 34:13-15 (Edom).
15: 'Until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high, and the wilderness becomes a fruitful orchard, and what is fruitful orchard is counted as forest' — the Spirit-outpouring as the reversal-agent: 'ad-yiare aleinu ruach mimmmarom, until the Spirit is poured/laid upon us from above.' The ruach (Spirit = the divine wind/breath/Spirit of Genesis 1:2 that hovered over the primordial chaos) 'poured from above' = the vertical descent of divine power. The agricultural reversal follows immediately: 'vehayah midbar lakarmel, and the wilderness will become a fruitful orchard.' Midbar (wilderness/desert) → karmel (fruitful garden-land). 'And the karmel will be counted as a forest' (vehakarmel laya'ar yekhashev, and the orchard will be reckoned as a forest) — the fruitful orchard becomes so abundantly productive it registers as a wild forest. Joel 2:28-29 expands this Spirit-outpouring; Peter cites Joel at Pentecost (Acts 2:17): 'In the last days, God says, I will pour out my Spirit on all people.'
16: 'Then justice will dwell in the wilderness, and righteousness remain in the fruitful orchard' — the ecological transformation producing social transformation: 'veshakhan bamidbar mishpat, and justice will dwell in the wilderness.' The midbar (wilderness) that was previously the domain of chaos and danger (the Negev through which Judean ambassadors traveled to Egypt at great risk, Isaiah 30:6) now becomes the habitat of mishpat (justice). 'And righteousness remain in the fruitful orchard' (utsedaqah bakarmel teshev, and righteousness will sit in the orchard). The double: justice in the wild place (wilderness) and righteousness in the cultivated place (orchard). The Spirit's transformation reaches both the uncultivated and cultivated domains of the land.
17: 'The fruit of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness will be quietness and confident security forever' — the righteousness → peace → security chain: 'vehayah ma'aseh hatsedaqah shalom, and the product of righteousness will be peace.' The three-stage sequence: tsedaqah (righteousness = the right ordering of covenant relationships) produces shalom (peace = the comprehensive well-being of the whole community). And the 'service/result of righteousness' (avoda hatsedaqah) = 'quietness and security forever' (hashqet uvetach ad-olam, quietness and confidence/security to the age). The betach (security = the same root as the misplaced trust of the Egypt-alliance: in Isaiah 30:15 'in quietness and trust shall be your strength' — now realized in the Spirit-era). When righteousness is the foundation, the quietness of 30:15 is finally available.
18: 'My people will dwell in a peaceful home, in secure dwellings, and in undisturbed resting places' — the threefold dwelling-security: 'veyashav ammi binaveh shalom, and my people will dwell in a home of peace.' Naveh shalom (home of peace = the peaceful pasture of a flock, the settled dwelling of a covenant community at rest). 'In secure dwellings' (umishshenot mibtachim, and in secure habitations — mibtachim from batach = the trustworthy/confident, the dwelling that can be relied upon). 'And in quiet resting places' (uvmenuchot sha'ananot, and in quiet resting places — menucha = the rest of Psalms 23:2: 'he leads me beside quiet waters... he makes me lie down in green pastures'). The triple description of the Spirit-era community: peaceful home, secure dwelling, quiet rest — the complete reversal of the fear, siege, and exile that characterized the Assyrian crisis.
19: 'Even when hail comes crashing through the forest and the city is utterly humbled and leveled' — the eschatological stability even in cosmic disturbance: 'uvarad beredet hayya'ar, and hail in the falling of the forest.' Even when the hailstorm of judgment crashes through the forest (the military image: the battle of Isaiah 30:30's hailstones destroying the Assyrian force), and 'the city is utterly humbled' (ubeshoflah tishpal ha'ir, and in the lowering the city will be humbled). The repeated shofal (brought low): both the forest and the city are humbled. Yet the covenant community's 'peaceful home, secure dwelling, quiet rest' (Isaiah 32:18) endure through the external turmoil. The surrounding destruction does not destabilize the Spirit-filled community's rest.
20: 'Blessed are you who sow beside every stream, who let your oxen and donkeys range freely' — the beatitude of the Spirit-era farmer: 'ashreikhem zor'e al-kol-mayim, blessed are you who sow beside all waters.' The ashre (blessed = the beatitude formula of Psalms 1:1; Matthew 5:3-12) applied to the farmer. 'Beside all waters' (al-kol-mayim = beside every body of water) = the unlimited water access of the Spirit-era (streams on every mountain in Isaiah 30:25; the poured-out Spirit of Isaiah 32:15). 'Who let your ox and donkey range freely' (meshalche regel hashhor vehacharor, who send freely the foot of the ox and the donkey) — the working animals roam without restriction, finding abundant pasture everywhere (Isaiah 30:23-24's 'spacious pastures' and 'salted fodder'). The Spirit-era blessing extends to the animals who labor for the community.
